body.label: real_matrix_5.root.thm
body.claim: Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix with entries in $\mathbb{R}$. If $A^2 = -I_n$, where $I_n$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix, then $n$ is even.

proof_steps.assumption: A is an n × n real matrix satisfying A^2 = -I_n.

proof_steps.claim: A^2 + I_n = 0 as a linear map on \mathbb{R}^n.
proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite the assumed matrix identity A^2 = -I_n by adding I_n to both sides.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: If a linear operator T satisfies p(T)=0, then its minimal polynomial m_T divides p.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Minimal polynomial divisibility property

proof_steps.claim: The minimal polynomial m_A(x) of A divides x^2+1.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use the defining divisibility property of the minimal polynomial.

proof_steps.claim: The polynomial x^2+1 has distinct complex roots i and -i.
proof_steps.proof_method: Compute its roots over C.

deduced_from_claim: The polynomial x^2+1 has distinct complex roots i and -i.
proof_steps.claim: The polynomial x^2+1 has no repeated roots.
proof_steps.proof_method: Distinct roots imply no repeated roots.

deduced_from_claim: The polynomial x^2+1 has no repeated roots.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: Every divisor of a square-free polynomial is square-free.
deduced_from_theorem.description: Used to pass from x^2+1 to its divisor m_A(x).

proof_steps.claim: Any divisor of x^2+1 has no repeated roots.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use that divisors of polynomials with no repeated roots also have no repeated roots.

deduced_from_claim: m_A(x) divides x^2+1.
Any divisor of x^2+1 has no repeated roots.
proof_steps.claim: The minimal polynomial m_A(x) of A has no repeated roots.
proof_steps.proof_method: Apply the preceding divisor statement to m_A(x).

deduced_from_claim: The minimal polynomial m_A(x) of A has no repeated roots.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: A matrix is diagonalizable over an algebraically closed field if and only if its minimal polynomial splits into distinct linear factors.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Diagonalizability criterion via minimal polynomial
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied after extending scalars to C.

proof_steps.claim: A is diagonalizable over C.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use the minimal-polynomial criterion for diagonalizability over C.

deduced_from_claim: A is diagonalizable over C.
proof_steps.claim: There exists a basis of C^n with respect to which the matrix of A is diagonal.
proof_steps.proof_method: Unpack the meaning of diagonalizability over C.

deduced_from_claim: The minimal polynomial m_A(x) divides x^2+1.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: Every eigenvalue of a linear operator is a root of its minimal polynomial.
deduced_from_theorem.description: Together with m_A(x) | x^2+1, this implies eigenvalues are roots of x^2+1.

proof_steps.claim: All eigenvalues of A over C are roots of x^2+1.
proof_steps.proof_method: Eigenvalues are roots of the minimal polynomial, and the minimal polynomial divides x^2+1.

deduced_from_claim: All eigenvalues of A over C are roots of x^2+1.
The roots of x^2+1 over C are i and -i.
proof_steps.claim: Every eigenvalue of A is either i or -i.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use the computed roots of x^2+1.

proof_steps.variable_type: the eigenvalues of A over C, listed with algebraic multiplicity

deduced_from_claim: Every eigenvalue of A is either i or -i.
proof_steps.claim: Each λ_j belongs to {i,-i}.
proof_steps.proof_method: Specialize the statement about all eigenvalues to the listed eigenvalues λ_1,…,λ_n.

proof_steps.variable_type: the number of indices j such that λ_j=i

deduced_from_claim: k is the number of indices j such that λ_j=i.
Each λ_j belongs to {i,-i}.
proof_steps.claim: The number of indices j such that λ_j=-i is n-k.
proof_steps.proof_method: The n listed eigenvalues are partitioned into those equal to i and those equal to -i.

deduced_from_claim: A is diagonalizable over C with eigenvalues λ_1,…,λ_n.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The determinant of a diagonalizable matrix equals the product of its eigenvalues, counted with algebraic multiplicity.

proof_steps.claim: det(A)=∏_{j=1}^n λ_j.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use the determinant of the diagonal form of A.

deduced_from_claim: det(A)=∏_{j=1}^n λ_j.
k eigenvalues are i and n-k eigenvalues are -i.
proof_steps.claim: det(A)=i^k\cdot(-i)^{n-k}.
proof_steps.proof_method: Group the eigenvalue product according to whether λ_j=i or λ_j=-i.

proof_steps.claim: det(A^2)=det(-I_n).
proof_steps.proof_method: Take determinants on both sides of A^2=-I_n.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For square matrices B and C of the same size, det(BC)=det(B)det(C).
deduced_from_theorem.name: Multiplicativity of determinant

proof_steps.claim: det(A^2)=(det A)^2.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use multiplicativity of the determinant.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For an n×n scalar matrix cI_n, det(cI_n)=c^n det(I_n), and det(I_n)=1.

proof_steps.proof_method: Compute the determinant of the scalar matrix -I_n.

deduced_from_claim: det(A^2)=det(-I_n).
det(A^2)=(det A)^2.
proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute the computed determinants into det(A^2)=det(-I_n).

deduced_from_claim: A has real entries.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The determinant of a real matrix is a real number.

proof_steps.claim: det A is a real number.
proof_steps.proof_method: A determinant is a polynomial expression in the entries of the matrix, so real entries give a real determinant.

deduced_from_claim: det A is a real number.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The square of a real number is nonnegative.

proof_steps.claim: (det A)^2 is a nonnegative real number.
proof_steps.proof_method: Square a real number.

deduced_from_claim: (det A)^2 is a nonnegative real number.
proof_steps.claim: (-1)^n is a nonnegative real number.
proof_steps.proof_method: Use the scalar equation (det A)^2=(-1)^n.

deduced_from_claim: (-1)^n is a nonnegative real number.
The only real values of (-1)^n are 1 and -1, and -1 is not nonnegative.
proof_steps.proof_method: Exclude the value -1 since it is not nonnegative.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For an integer n, (-1)^n=1 if and only if n is even.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Parity characterization by powers of -1
deduced_from_theorem.description: Used to convert the sign equation into parity.

proof_steps.claim: n is an even integer.
proof_steps.proof_method: Apply the basic parity characterization of powers of -1.

proof.text:
Assume that $A$ is an $n \times n$ real matrix satisfying $A^2 = -I_n$. The goal is to prove that $n$ is an even integer.

First, consider $A$ as a linear operator on the real vector space $\mathbb{R}^n$. The equation $A^2 = -I_n$ implies that, as a linear map, $A$ satisfies the polynomial equation
\[
A^2 + I_n = 0.
\]
Equivalently, the minimal polynomial $m_A(x)$ of $A$ divides the polynomial
\[
p(x) := x^2 + 1.
\]

The polynomial $x^2 + 1$ has distinct complex roots, namely $i$ and $-i$. Therefore $x^2 + 1$ has no repeated roots. Consequently, any divisor of $x^2+1$ also has no repeated roots. Hence the minimal polynomial $m_A(x)$ of $A$ has no repeated roots.

A real matrix whose minimal polynomial has no repeated roots is diagonalizable over $\mathbb{C}$. Thus, after extending scalars from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{C}$, there exists a basis of $\mathbb{C}^n$ with respect to which the matrix of $A$ is diagonal. In particular, all eigenvalues of $A$ (over $\mathbb{C}$) are roots of the polynomial $x^2 + 1$, hence every eigenvalue of $A$ is either $i$ or $-i$.

Let $\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n$ denote the eigenvalues of $A$ over $\mathbb{C}$, listed with algebraic multiplicity. Then each $\lambda_j$ belongs to the set $\{i,-i\}$. Let $k$ denote the number of indices $j$ such that $\lambda_j = i$. Then the number of indices $j$ such that $\lambda_j = -i$ is $n-k$.

Consider the determinant of $A$ viewed as a complex matrix. Since $A$ is diagonalizable over $\mathbb{C}$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$, the determinant of $A$ equals the product of its eigenvalues:
\[
\det(A) = \prod_{j=1}^n \lambda_j = i^k \cdot (-i)^{n-k}.
\]
On the other hand, the matrix identity $A^2 = -I_n$ implies a relation for the determinant. Taking determinants on both sides and using multiplicativity of the determinant, we obtain
\[
\det(A^2) = \det(-I_n).
\]
The left-hand side is
\[
\det(A^2) = (\det A)^2.
\]
The right-hand side is
\[
\det(-I_n) = (-1)^n \det(I_n) = (-1)^n.
\]
Thus we obtain the scalar equation
\[
(\det A)^2 = (-1)^n.
\]

Since $A$ has real entries, its determinant $\det A$ is a real number. Therefore $(\det A)^2$ is a nonnegative real number. Hence $(-1)^n$ must be a nonnegative real number. This forces $(-1)^n = 1$, because the only real values of $(-1)^n$ are $1$ and $-1$, and the value $-1$ is not nonnegative. Thus
\[
(-1)^n = 1.
\]
By the basic properties of integer parity, the equality $(-1)^n = 1$ holds if and only if $n$ is an even integer.

Therefore $n$ is even.
